query: relevance of blood sugar estimation in neonates

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hyperglycemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Considerations for workup should include: Serum glucose: Relying on point-of-care blood glucose testing alone yields erroneous results. A high blood glucose level must be verified by measuring the serum glucose level before starting treatment for neonatal hyperglycemia. Venous blood glucose measurement is preferred compared to capillary blood glucose measurement. Capillary blood glucose (heel stick sample) is 15% lower, and the hematocrit value affects the levels. Urine glucose 2+ or higher suggests osmotic diuresis. Complete blood count and C-reactive protein help in ruling out sepsis. Monitor serum electrolyte levels in patients with hyperglycemia. The osmotic diuresis causes electrolyte loss in urine. Measure the weight of the baby to determine hydration status. If hyperglycemia is persistent, serum insulin level, serum, and urine C-peptide levels are used to rule out monogenic diabetes and Type 1 diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hypoglycemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Point-of-care testing (POCT) offers a quick and cost-effective method for screening for hypoglycemia. [5] However, these methods have limitations. Most standard instruments use non-enzymatic methods to measure blood glucose concentration, which are less accurate at lower glucose values than laboratory analysis using glucose oxidase methods (the gold standard). [5] Whole blood samples (used in POCT) have 10% to 18% lower glucose concentrations than plasma, depending on the hematocrit. [5] Therefore, abnormally low glucose values on POCT require confirmation by measuring plasma glucose concentration using clinical laboratory methods. [4] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hypoglycemia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The CHYLD study is a prospective cohort investigation of infants born >32 weeks gestation with risk factors for neonatal hypoglycemia. [19] The initial findings showed that treatment of neonatal hypoglycemia to maintain blood glucose >47 mg/dL did not correlate with an increased risk of neurosensory impairment at 4.5 years of age. [19] Infants that experienced more severe or prolonged episodes of low blood glucose did not have worse outcomes. [5] [19] Investigators could not establish a numerical glucose level at which risk increased, but found that glucose instability (the proportion of measurements and duration of time blood glucose values were outside the range of 54 to 72 mg/dL) in the 48 hours of life was most predictive of worse developmental outcomes. [5] [19] Infants with the greatest glucose instability had a 2 to 3-fold increased risk of neurosensory impairment. [5] [19] Interestingly, moderate "hyperglycemia," blood glucose values >72 mg/dL, did correlate with an increased risk of poor visual motor and executive functioning. [19] This finding raises the question: are infants experiencing low and "high" early blood glucose values at risk of worse neurodevelopmental outcomes?

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hypoglycemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: More recently, the use of continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) in the detection and management of neonatal hypoglycemia is under investigation. A study published in 2010 by Harris et al. looked at the usefulness of continuous glucose monitoring in 102 infants >32 weeks gestation at risk of hypoglycemia during the first 7 days of life. [14] Infants were screened for hypoglycemia with intermittent blood glucose measurements and started on early oral feeds or intravenous dextrose solution per clinical guidelines. [14] Investigators found that detected hypoglycemia (blood glucose <47 mg/dL) was present in 44% of infants using continuous glucose monitoring, versus 32% of infants with intermittent blood glucose sampling. [14] And that there is good agreement between interstitial (used in continuous monitoring) and blood glucose measurements. [14] The study suggests that continuous glucose monitoring is a safe, easy to use, and detects more episodes of hypoglycemia. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hypoglycemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A 1988 multicenter nutritional study by Lucas et al. suggested a blood glucose concentration <47 mg/dL as the critical threshold associated with adverse neurodevelopmental outcomes. [1] [2] [5] [6] The study looked at blood glucose levels in 661 preterm infants with a birth weight of <1850 grams enrolled in a nutritional study investigating early feeding strategies and cognitive outcomes. [1] [6] Investigators found that the number of days of blood glucose concentrations <47 mg/dL associated with lower motor and mental developmental scores on the Bayley Scales of Infant Development at 18 months corrected age. [6] Infants that experienced hypoglycemia (glucose <47 mg/dL) on 5 or greater days had 3 to 5 times increased risk of neurodevelopmental impairment. [6] The authors concluded that "even moderate hypoglycemia is associated with a considerable increase in adverse neurodevelopmental sequelae" and called for a reevaluation of the then current practice trends. As a result, this value of '47 mg/dL' became widely accepted as the standard numerical value to define neonatal hypoglycemia for all infants, even healthy, term, appropriate for gestational age infants. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hypoglycemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Despite a better understanding of early glucose homeostasis and transitional hypoglycemia in the first 48 hours of life, gaps in our knowledge persist. Observations have shown that healthy infants experience transient hypoglycemia as a part of the normal adaption to extrauterine life, with a decline in blood glucose concentrations to values as low as 20 to 25 mg/dL in the first two hours of life. [1] However, because we do not routinely measure blood glucose concentrations in healthy infants without risk factors for hypoglycemia, it is difficult to define 'normal' levels of blood glucose in the first 48 hours of life. As Harding et al. question: "even if healthy infants experience low glucose concentrations, can we extend these values to infants at risk of impaired metabolic adaption?" [5] And how low is too low?

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hyperglycemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hyperglycemia is usually defined as serum glucose greater than 150 mg/dl (8.3 mmol/L) or whole blood glucose greater than 125 mg/dl (6.9 mmol/L), irrespective of gestational or postmenstrual age. Usually, the safe target for a neonate’s blood glucose level is 70 to 150 mg/dl. [1] The cut-off for a safe target is based on the renal glucose threshold of preterm neonates.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hypoglycemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: It is clear that extremely low blood glucose concentrations in neonates can cause apnea, irritability, lethargy, seizures, [7] [8] and brain damage [9] ; and that prolonged or symptomatic hypoglycemia may correlate with long-term neurodevelopmental deficits. [1] [10] However, the long-term significance of early, asymptomatic and transiently low glucose levels remain not well established. And the evidence to support a clear numerical value of blood glucose that is associated with brain injury or reliably predicts adverse neurodevelopmental outcomes is lacking. [3] [2] Even Lucas et al. acknowledged that 'the association between [blood glucose concentrations <47 mg/dL] and poor neurodevelopmental outcomes might not be causal and might reflect a failure to adjust for confounding factors.' [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hypoglycemia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Despite requests for further research of infants at risk of hypoglycemia with long-term neurodevelopmental follow up, [17] [18] there have only been two studies that meet the methodological criteria recommended by Boluyt et al.: A 15-year follow-up study of recurrent hypoglycemia in preterm infants by Tin et al. [15] and The Children with Hypoglycemia and Their Later Development (CHYLD) study. [5] [19] Published in 2012, the Tin et al. study sought to confirm the findings of the 1988 Lucas et al. study. [6] This prospective study included preterm infants <32 weeks gestation born between 1990 and 1991 who had blood glucose measurements taken in the first 10 days of life. [15] Investigators matched infants that had experienced recurrent neonatal hypoglycemia (defined as blood glucose <47 mg/dL on 3 or more days in the first 10 days of life) with euglycemia controls and found no differences in development or physical disability at age 2 years. [2] [15] On reassessment at age 15 years, investigators again found no difference in development or IQ scores between the two groups. [15] The authors concluded that the "study found no evidence to support the belief that recurrent low blood glucose levels in the first 10 days of life usually pose a hazard to preterm infants". [15]

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hyperglycemia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hyperglycemia, particularly in preterm neonates, is usually associated with an increased risk of mortality and morbidity. The severity of impact increases with prolonged hyperglycemia. Prompt evaluation and management of neonatal hyperglycemia decrease the impact.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hyperglycemia -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Increased mortality risk among preterm and VLBW infants

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hyperglycemia -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hyperglycemia is a critical problem in premature infants due to the relatively immature mechanisms to handle glucose equilibrium. The parents of preterm and small gestational-age neonates need appropriate counseling as they are worried about frequent blood glucose monitoring. The healthcare team should explain the short-term and long-term impacts of neonatal hyperglycemia to the parents.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gluconeogenesis -- Clinical Significance -- Hypoglycemia in the Preterm Infant
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm infants are particularly at risk of developing hypoglycemia. Low-birth-weight neonates have limited glycogen and fat stores but also express gluconeogenic enzymes at suboptimal levels. Preterm infants' energy stores can diminish quickly, as they cannot mount an adequate counterregulatory response. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hypoglycemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Hypoglycemia is the most common metabolic disturbance occurring in the neonatal period. Screening at-risk infants and the management of low blood glucose levels in the first hours to days of life is a frequent issue in the care of the newborn infant. Yet, a clear definition of neonatal hypoglycemia is lacking. Current screening guidelines and management algorithms are based on limited evidence, relying more on expert opinion to guide recommendations. This activity reviews neonatal hypoglycemia and the need to develop an improved knowledge of it. This activity highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the recognition and management of neonatal hypoglycemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance that arises during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication within the broader category of complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. This condition represents a distinct obstetric complication where altered glucose metabolism adversely affects maternal and fetal health, underscoring the importance of monitoring and managing glucose levels throughout gestation, delivery, and the postpartum period.

Recognizing these interconnections, the associated syndromes, and the relevant laboratory parameters (e.g., total hemoglobin) is essential for accurate diagnosis, interpretation of test results, and targeted management of infants during the neonatal and early infancy periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2306
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium. Among the many obstetric complications encompassed by this larger category, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium represents a specific type of complication. This relationship underscores that gestational diabetes and related disorders of glucose metabolism are recognized as important pregnancy‑related complications and are included in the overall spectrum of conditions that can affect maternal and fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period. Effective identification and management of these glucose‑related complications are essential components of comprehensive obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). Laboratory findings that fall below reference ranges, particularly low total hemoglobin concentration, are interpreted as indicative of hemolytic or perinatal jaundice, underscoring the diagnostic importance of quantitative testing in this age group. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that the neonatal period serves as a critical temporal nexus linking infectious, metabolic, hematologic, and hemorrhagic conditions—many organized under “other perinatal conditions” or “hemolytic‑jaundice” categories—and that recognizing these interconnections is essential for accurate assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma and DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) are both classified as specific forms of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, the broader category of neonatal disorders. The condition Cerebral injury due to birth trauma represents a narrower, trauma‑related neurological injury that occurs in the newborn period, while transient neonatal diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disorder that appears shortly after birth and typically resolves. Recognizing that both entities fall under the umbrella of neonatal disorders highlights the need for targeted assessment and management strategies tailored to the unique pathophysiology of each condition in newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several specific pancreatic endocrine disorders. Among its children are ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which describes the elevated blood‑glucose levels that characterize hyperglycemia; مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which refers to diabetes that has not yet produced secondary complications; فرط أنسولين الدم, indicating an excess of circulating insulin often seen in early insulin‑resistance states or insulin‑producing tumors; and ورم غلوكاغوني, a rare glucagon‑secreting tumor (glucagonoma) that can also cause hyperglycemia. These conditions are interrelated through their impact on pancreatic hormone production and regulation of blood glucose, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of pancreatic endocrine function in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1440
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all classified as اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, a neonatal disorder. This grouping indicates that both forms of transient neonatal diabetes mellitus and the syndrome that combines developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes fall under the broader category of neonatal disorders, highlighting their importance in newborn assessment, monitoring, and specialized management.

Laboratory evaluation, particularly measurement of total hemoglobin concentration, is interpreted against these interrelated conditions to guide diagnosis, risk stratification, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.content: each condition as a potential adverse event, enabling systematic risk assessment, targeted monitoring, operative decision‑making, and coordinated maternal‑fetal and newborn care aimed at reducing morbidity and improving outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1640
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these specific neonatal diabetes conditions and the syndrome combining developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes are recognized as forms or manifestations of diabetes. In addition, the condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is an inverse‑isa relationship to مرض السكري, meaning that gestational or peripartum diabetes represents a particular clinical presentation of the disease. Clinically, مرض السكري is associated with pancreatic disorders (not diabetes), reflecting that patients with diabetes may also exhibit other pancreatic abnormalities unrelated to the primary diabetic process. Moreover, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (a retinal disorder) and اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), underscoring the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse forms of diabetes—including transient neonatal types and pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance—and their broader systemic associations with pancreatic and retinal pathology, which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice, also referred to by the Arabic term “يرقان وليدي,” can occur during the fetal and/or neonatal period and may be observed throughout the broader period of life between birth and death, including the specific stage of infancy. In clinical assessment, a total hemoglobin concentration that falls below the reference range is interpreted as indicative of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice, and the measurement of total hemoglobin concentration is used to interpret the presence of this condition. Recognizing the timing of occurrence, the laboratory interpretation of low hemoglobin, and the synonymous terminology enhances patient evaluation and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2434
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is a broad category that encompasses specific maternal health problems such as hypertension and/or vomiting complicating pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium, as well as diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance occurring during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium. Both hypertension‑related vomiting and gestational diabetes represent distinct subtypes of the overall complications associated with the perinatal period, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and targeted management of these conditions to protect maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1089
retrieved_docs.content: with or lead to infectious complications around birth. Overall, the knowledge network portrays neonatal disorders as an interconnected group of conditions that include traumatic cerebral injury, intracranial and neonatal hemorrhage, perinatal infections, metabolic diseases such as transient neonatal diabetes, neurologic problems like developmental delay and epilepsy, and extreme prematurity. Birth trauma is a key antecedent of intracranial bleeding, which is classified under neonatal hemorrhage, while perinatal infection serves both as a parent condition for unspecified perinatal disorders and as a member of the broader neonatal disorder set. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns presenting with any of these interrelated pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1740
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis/Diseases Component is classified under مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). The specific disorders DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are both mapped to مرض السكري, indicating they are forms of transient neonatal diabetes that fall within the broader category of diabetes mellitus. The combined condition Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes is also mapped to مرض السكري, highlighting its association with neonatal diabetes as part of the diabetes spectrum. Clinically, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Additionally, متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome) is identified as a child of مرض السكري, signifying that metabolic syndrome can arise as a downstream metabolic complication of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate how various neonatal diabetes subtypes, developmental and neurological manifestations, ocular complications, and metabolic syndrome are interconnected within the overarching framework of diabetes mellitus, underscoring the importance of comprehensive assessment and management in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is classified under the broader category Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery, indicating that it is a subset of the clinical indications guiding obstetric management. Within this group of obstetric complications, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication, highlighting that gestational diabetes and related glucose intolerance are important conditions to consider when evaluating and treating patients during pregnancy, labor, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the network of relationships portrays diabetes as a multifaceted condition that influences and is influenced by genetic abnormalities, neonatal presentations, limb and ocular complications, metabolic comorbidities, diagnostic testing, and medication safety, all of which are essential considerations for comprehensive patient management.
